did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) to classify images of seven different skin diseases (including skin cancer types like melanoma and basal cell carcinoma) from a dataset of 10,000 images. The model achieved an accuracy of 80% to 86%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a 'SkinSight AI' system using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Explainable AI (XAI). It details a specific methodology (data preprocessing, model architecture with 13 layers, SHAP/LIME implementation), uses a specific dataset (PLCO dataset, 10,000 images), and reports original results (accuracy 80-86%). Although published in a conference proceedings (CISCT), it contains original research results rather than being a narrative review or survey.

title: SkinSight AI: Skin Cancer Prediction system using Deep Learning and Explainable Artificial Intelligence
journal: 2024 4th International Conference on Innovative Sustainable Computational Technologies (CISCT)
brief_summary: This study developed the SkinSight AI system to classify seven types of skin diseases, including melanoma and basal cell carcinoma, using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI). The model was trained on a dataset of 10,000 images from the National Cancer Institute's PLCO dataset, which included metadata such as age, sex, and lesion localization. The CNN architecture consisted of 13 layers designed to capture hierarchical features from the dermatological images. Evaluation metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score were computed, with the model achieving an accuracy range of 80% to 86% (specifically 86.70% on the test set). XAI techniques, specifically SHAP and LIME, were integrated to enhance interpretability by highlighting feature importance for clinical decision-making. The results demonstrate that the multilayered CNN combined with XAI provides a precise and interpretable tool for skin disease prediction.

all_task_types.target: Seven different skin disease categories (AKIEC, VASC, BCC, MEL, DF, NV, BKL)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the aim is to 'classify images depicting seven different skin diseases' using a CNN with a SoftMax activation function in the output layer for multi-class classification. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels for skin lesions.

datasets_used.name: PLCO dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset from the National Cancer Institute containing 10,015 images of skin lesions collected from clinics and healthcare centers globally. It includes metadata such as age, sex, and anatomical location. The images are in JPEG format with a resolution of 600x450 pixels.

use.training: Used for training the CNN model (80% of the data).
use.testing: Used for testing the model (20% of the data).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score.

PLCO_dataset.notes: The model achieved an overall accuracy of 86.70% on the test set. Class 4 (Dermatofibroma) had the highest support (1672) and performance. Class 3 (Melanoma) had zero precision, recall, and F1 score with only 29 samples. The validation accuracy was reported as 84.17% in the text, though Table III shows a macro average accuracy of 78% which may refer to the confusion matrix visualization or a different calculation context; the text explicitly states 'test accuracy is 86.82%' in one sentence and '86.70%' in another, with 1903/2481 equating to 76.73% if taken literally, but the text claims 86.70%. The table III macro average accuracy is listed as 0.78. The text states 'accuracy 0.78' in the table row for 'accuracy' but then says 'test accuracy is 86.70%'. There is a discrepancy in the paper's reported numbers. The table lists 'accuracy 0.78' for the test set in the summary row, but the text below says 'accuracy of 86.70%'. We report the explicit percentage given in the text for the final result.

conclusions: The SkinSight AI system, utilizing Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI), demonstrated good precision and consistent performance in classifying seven different skin diseases, with an accuracy range of 80% to 86%.

The integration of XAI techniques (specifically SHAP) enhances the interpretability of the CNN model, allowing clinicians to understand the decision-making process and feature importance, thereby addressing the 'black box' limitation of traditional AI.

The multilayered CNN architecture (13 layers) effectively captures hierarchical features from dermatological images, enabling accurate identification of skin diseases including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and others.

The study indicates that AI models can surpass human accuracy in diagnosis by analyzing detailed features from images, offering a tool for quick and accurate identification of skin diseases, particularly beneficial for remote regions with limited access to dermatological treatment.

Data augmentation techniques (adding noise, rotation, flipping) and the use of a diverse dataset (PLCO dataset with 10,000 images) contributed to the model's robustness and ability to discern nuanced patterns in skin lesions.
important_remarks: Significant class imbalance exists in the dataset, with Class 4 (Dermatofibroma) having 1,672 samples while Class 3 (Melanoma) has only 29 samples, leading to poor performance for melanoma (0 precision, recall, and F1 score).

The paper reports contradictory accuracy figures: the text claims 86.70% accuracy (1903/2481 correct), while Table III lists an accuracy of 0.78 (78%) for the test set; 1903/2481 actually equals ~76.7%, indicating potential calculation or reporting errors.

The model achieved zero predictive capability for Melanoma (Class 3) due to the extremely small sample size (n=29), raising concerns about the clinical utility of the system for this critical cancer type.
The study lacks external validation, relying solely on the PLCO dataset split into training (80%) and testing (20%) sets, which limits generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

Data leakage risk is present as the PLCO dataset contains metadata (age, sex, localization) which may correlate with disease prevalence, potentially allowing the model to learn shortcuts rather than purely visual features.
The dataset is skewed towards younger demographics (maximum number of people aged 45 and below) and male subjects, limiting the model's applicability to older or female populations.
The paper does not report standard deviation or confidence intervals for the performance metrics, making it difficult to assess the statistical significance or robustness of the results.

The CNN architecture (13 layers) was custom-built rather than using established pre-trained models, and no ablation study or comparison with state-of-the-art models (beyond brief literature mentions) is provided to justify its superiority.

The study claims to use Explainable AI (SHAP/LIME) but provides limited quantitative evidence of interpretability, focusing mainly on feature importance plots without validating if the explanations align with clinical ground truth.

The dataset includes images from various sources (DSLR, mobile devices) with inconsistent lighting and resolution, which may introduce noise and affect model robustness in real-world clinical settings.
